summary: This notebook demonstrates a practical approach to optimizing text sequences by minimizing their perplexity using a frozen large language model. It implements a custom PerplexityCalculator that leverages the transformers library to load Gemma-2-9B and compute sequence loss efficiently with an LRU cache. The author then applies a brute-force word permutation strategy to iteratively rearrange text segments, aiming to find lower-perplexity variants. The key takeaway is that fixed LLMs can serve as effective, differentiable-free scorers for text optimization, and caching mechanisms are crucial for managing inference costs during iterative search. Readers learn how to adapt open-weight models for metric-driven text refinement without training.
competition_flow: Loads a sample submission, computes text perplexity using a cached Gemma-2-9B model, optimizes text via brute-force word permutations to minimize perplexity, and saves the results.
data_reading: Reads sample_submission.csv via pd.read_csv; manually constructs a text column from a hardcoded multi-line string.
data_processing: Splits the hardcoded string by newlines to populate the text column; no additional cleaning or normalization is applied.
insights: Caching model outputs with an LRU structure drastically reduces inference latency during iterative optimization.
Brute-forcing local word permutations can effectively minimize sequence perplexity without requiring gradient-based fine-tuning.
Frozen open-weight LLMs can serve as reliable, fixed scorers for text quality and coherence in competition tasks.
